title: 🎯 Optimize Memory Reclamation and Mitigate Latency Spikes During Large-Scale Key Expiration Cycles
body:
## 📝 Description During periods of high-volume key expirations (specifically when millions of keys or large complex data types like Hashes, Sets, and Sorted Sets expire simultaneously), the system experiences significant memory growth (RSS bloat) and latency spikes. This issue stems from two primary bottlenecks in the expiration engine: 1. **Synchronous Deallocation Overhead:** When the active expiration cycle (`activeExpireCycle`) runs, deleting large keys synchronously blocks the main event loop, causing command processing latency to spike. 2. **Delayed Memory Reclamation:** The memory allocator (e.g., jemalloc) does not return freed memory to the operating system immediately, leading to a high memory fragmentation ratio and temporary memory exhaustion under heavy write/expire workloads. We need to optimize the active expiration algorithm to pace itself under memory pressure and ensure large keys are safely delegated to background threads for lazy freeing. ## 🎯 Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Active expiration (`activeExpireCycle`) must not block the main event loop for longer than the configured CPU time limit (default 25% of CPU time per iteration, or configured via `active-expire-effort`). - [ ] Expired keys containing large nested structures (elements > 64, configurable via `lazyfree-lazy-expire`) must be freed asynchronously in a background thread using `dbAsyncDelete` instead of blocking the main thread. - [ ] Implement adaptive pacing in the active expiration cycle: if memory fragmentation or RSS exceeds a critical threshold, the cycle should yield earlier to prevent memory exhaustion. - [ ] Ensure memory metrics (`used_memory` and `used_memory_rss` in `INFO memory`) return to baseline levels within a deterministic timeframe post-expiration without memory leaks. ## 🛠️ Technical Specifications & Context The core of the expiration logic resides in the following files: * **`src/expire.c`**: Contains `activeExpireCycle()` which samples keys with TTLs and deletes expired ones. * **`src/lazyfree.c`**: Contains the asynchronous deletion logic (`dbAsyncDelete`, `freeObjAsync`) which offloads memory deallocation to bio (Background I/O) threads. * **`src/db.c`**: Contains `dbSyncDelete` and general key space deletion routines. ### Recommended Changes: 1. **Enhance `activeExpireCycle` in `src/expire.c`**: * Check if `lazyfree-lazy-expire` is enabled before deleting a key. If enabled, evaluate the object size/type and route the deletion through `dbAsyncDelete` instead of `dbSyncDelete`. * Monitor memory usage during the active expire loop. If `zmalloc_used_memory()` exceeds the configured `maxmemory` limit during the cycle, prioritize freeing memory over maintaining the target expiration rate to prevent Out-Of-Memory (OOM) panics. 2. **Optimize Allocator Release**: * If using jemalloc, trigger `mallctl("arena.g.purge", ...)` or `zmalloc_purge()` periodically when a large batch of keys is expired to force the allocator to release dirty pages back to the OS. ## 🧪 Verification & Testing To verify the fix, perform the following test scenario: 1. **Setup Test Data**: * Write a script to populate the database with 1,000,000 keys, each containing a Hash with 100 fields. * Set a uniform TTL on all keys to expire in 60 seconds (e.g., `EXPIRE key 60`). 2. **Monitor Performance**: * Run `redis-cli --latency-dist` or monitor command latency during the expiration window. * Query `INFO memory` every 1 second to track `used_memory`, `used_memory_rss`, and `mem_fragmentation_ratio`. 3. **Expected Results**: * Latency spikes during the expiration window must remain under 5ms. * `used_memory_rss` must decrease monotonically back to baseline (< 50MB idle) within 10 seconds after all keys have expired. * No OOM errors or crashes should occur during the expiration cycle. --- [![Opire Bounty](https://img.shields.io/badge/bounty-Opire-emerald?style=for-the-badge)](https://github.com/zcharliebeer/Redis)
